channel.title: Posts on Andre Walter
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Andre Walter

item.title: Estimation of Long Run Effects in R
item.description: A lot of quantitative data in political science comes in the form of “Time-Series Cross-Sectional” (TSCS) datasets. Such datasets usually consist of a low number of cross-sectional units (e.g. states, countries) and higher number of time periods. Since the introduction of panel-corrected standard errors in political science in 1995 by Beck and Katz, political science scholars have discussed the merits and pitfalls of TSCS data. One controversial aspect is the inclusion of lagged dependent variables (LDVs) in estimation equations.

item.title: A Plain Markdown Post
item.description: This sample post is mainly for blogdown users. If you do not use blogdown, you can skip the first section. To know more about the Hugo XMag theme, please read the About page. 1. Markdown or R Markdown This is a post written in plain Markdown (*.md) instead of R Markdown (*.Rmd). The major differences are: You cannot run any R code in a plain Markdown document, whereas in an R Markdown document, you can embed R code chunks (```{r}); A plain Markdown post is rendered through Blackfriday, and an R Markdown document is compiled by rmarkdown and Pandoc.

item.title: Swiss Cantonal Governments 1848-2017
item.description: R Markdown I’m currently working with Patrick Emmenegger on creating a database on the ideological composition of cantonal governments. We were able to compile a time series for most cantons starting in the mid 19th century. A complete Time-Series Cross-Section is available for all cantons for 1894 onwards (except Appenzell Inner-Rhodes which starts 1928 and, of course, Jura). Government Voliatility
